turns.user_message: I don't know, I just got tired of feeling guilty about smoking, I guess. And my friends were all bugging me about it. But then we went out for drinks and they were all smoking, and...
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for about 10 years, on and off. I've tried to quit a few times, but it never seems to stick. I just quit again about 6 weeks ago, but I only lasted 2 weeks before I started again.
Therapist: I see. Congratulations on quitting for 2 weeks, by the way! That's not easy. What made you want to quit this time, and what do you think triggered your relapse?
Patient: I don't know, I just got tired of feeling guilty about smoking, I guess. And my friends were all bugging me about it. But then we went out for drinks and they were all smoking, and...
Current patient message:
I don't know, I just got tired of feeling guilty about smoking, I guess. And my friends were all bugging me about it. But then we went out for drinks and they were all smoking, and...
Therapist:
step7_response: It sounds like feeling guilty and peer pressure were strong motivators for you to try quitting this time. What do you think about quitting for your own health - is that a reason that resonates with you?

turns.user_message: It's just really hard, you know? They don't get it. They're all like, "Oh, just have one, it's no big deal." But it is a big deal! And they don't understand why I'm trying to quit.
step2_rag_chunks: It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

relevant_strategies.outcome: only lasted 2 weeks before I started again
